flashcards.front: What header file is required for file I/O operations in C++?
flashcards.back: The <fstream> header file provides all file I/O classes including ifstream, ofstream, and fstream.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ifstream and ofstream?
flashcards.back: ifstream (input file stream) is used for reading from files, while ofstream (output file stream) is used for writing to files.

flashcards.front: What are the different file open modes in C++?
flashcards.back: ios::app (append), ios::ate (at end), ios::trunc (truncate), ios::in (read), ios::out (write), ios::binary (binary mode).

flashcards.front: How do you check if a file was opened successfully?
flashcards.back: Use the is_open() method on the file stream object, which returns true if the file is successfully opened.

flashcards.front: What is the default mode for ifstream?
flashcards.back: The default mode for ifstream is ios::in (read mode).

flashcards.front: What is the default mode for ofstream?
flashcards.back: The default mode for ofstream is ios::out | ios::trunc (write mode, truncates existing file).

flashcards.front: How do you write a line of text to a file using ofstream?
flashcards.back: Use the insertion operator (<<) with newline character: file << "Hello World" << endl;

flashcards.front: How do you read a line from a file using ifstream?
flashcards.back: Use getline() function: getline(file, variable) reads an entire line into a string.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of ios::app file mode?
flashcards.back: ios::app (append mode) positions the file pointer at the end of the file before each write operation, preserving existing content.

flashcards.front: How do you close a file in C++?
flashcards.back: Call the close() method on the file stream object: file.close();
fstream provides both input and output capabilities, allowing simultaneous reading and writing operations on a file.

flashcards.front: How do you read a single word from a file?
flashcards.back: Use the extraction operator (>>) with ifstream: file >> word; this reads whitespace-separated tokens.

flashcards.front: What happens if you open a file in ios::trunc mode that already exists?
flashcards.back: The existing file is deleted and a new empty file is created.

flashcards.front: What is the seekg() function used for?
flashcards.back: seekg() positions the get pointer (reading position) in a file. seegp(0) moves to beginning, seekp() positions the put pointer (writing).
